aliases: Korean Seafood Spicy Noodle Soup with Crab

english: Korean Seafood Spicy Noodle Soup with Crab

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Jjampong. Mapped to the broader 'noodle soup (food product)' class as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific Korean spicy seafood noodle soup term is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soup, noodle-based — used as closest FoodEx2 analogue for Korean spicy seafood noodle soup (Jjampong). No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this dish; this is an approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Jjampong or Korean spicy seafood noodle soup. A0FJP (noodle-based soup) is the closest defensible category code available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Jjampong or Korean spicy seafood noodle soup as of 2026.

serving.description: 1 bowl with noodles and side dishes
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Crab and Seafood Jjampong is approximately 700 g, comprising a large bowl of spicy seafood broth with wheat noodles, mixed seafood (crab, mussels, prawns, squid), and vegetables, typically accompanied by small side dishes (banchan).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Soup, seafood-based composite and noodle entries used as closest approved analogues for Korean spicy seafood noodle soup (Jjampong); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database for sodium and micronutrient validation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore entry exists for Jjampong (Korean spicy seafood noodle soup). Nutrient values from the input record (provenance: GrabFood AI-generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entries for seafood-based soups (FDC ID 172945 — Soup, seafood chowder) and cooked enriched noodles (FDC ID 168462), and against HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database entries for comparable mixed seafood noodle dishes. The input values are broadly consistent with these approved reference composites and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 400 mg/100 g is consistent with a broth-heavy seafood noodle dish. Trans fat at 0.07 g/100 g is a plausible trace level from seafood and cooking oils. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content are not reported in the input and are set to -1 (data not available from approved reference composites at this level of specificity for this dish).

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on wheat noodle soup (GI ~52) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Crab and Seafood Jjampong. GI is estimated at 52 based on the dominant carbohydrate source — wheat noodles — using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodle soup (GI ~52). Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 52 falls in the low category. The seafood, broth, and vegetable components do not materially raise the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 11.43 g/100g × 700 g) / 100 / 100 = (52 × 80.01) / 100 ≈ 41.6. Although GI is low, the GL per full 700 g serving is very high due to the large portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 400 mg/100 g; a full 700 g serving delivers approximately 2,800 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and gochujang, ask for broth served on the side to limit intake, and avoid sodium-dense banchan such as kimchi and pickled radish; even with modifications, portion size should be limited to no more than 300–350 g to keep sodium below 1,200 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles contribute 11.43 g carbohydrates per 100 g with only 1.14 g dietary fibre; while the GI is low (~52), the glycemic load per standard 700 g serving is very high (~41.6), significantly elevating postprandial blood glucose risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute wheat noodles with konjac or shirataki noodles to reduce glycemic load, limit portion to no more than 300 g, increase the proportion of seafood and non-starchy vegetables, and request gochujang to be used sparingly to reduce added sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100 g and saturated fat at 2.14 g/100 g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; however, seafood provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and the dish is relatively low in total fat (5.0 g/100 g) with only trace trans fat (0.07 g/100 g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce sodium and saturated fat exposure, request reduced seasoning and broth, and note that the seafood components (crab, mussels, prawns, squid) provide cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids that partially offset the sodium risk when the dish is consumed in a controlled portion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Seafood provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; however, gochugaru and gochujang are nightshade-derived and high sodium from broth and condiments may worsen tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3 content of the mixed seafood but should monitor joint response to nightshade-derived chili products and request reduced sodium seasoning to limit RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab and Seafood Jjampong (크랩 해물 짬뽕; romanized: Keuraeb Haemul Jjampong) is a Korean spicy seafood noodle soup dish featuring a fiery, deeply flavoured broth made with gochugaru (Korean red chili flakes), gochujang (Korean red chili paste), soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and a rich seafood stock. The dish is loaded with mixed seafood — typically crab, mussels, prawns, and squid — along with wheat noodles, cabbage, zucchini, and onion, finished with sesame oil. The broth is characteristically deep red, intensely spicy, and umami-rich, with charred and toasted seafood aromas from the stir-frying step before the broth is added. The noodles are springy and absorb the bold flavours of the soup. Jjampong originated in Korea and is one of the most popular Korean-Chinese (Jajangmyeon-style) noodle dishes; this crab-forward variant is commonly served in Korean restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 121 kcal, 7.86 g protein, 5.0 g fat (2.14 g saturated), 11.43 g carbohydrates, 1.14 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. Clinically, the high sodium content (approximately 2,800 mg per standard 700 g serving) makes this dish a significant concern for patients with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The wheat noodle base has a low glycemic index (~52) but produces a very high glycemic load per serving (~41.6) due to the large portion size, making portion control critical for patients with Type 2 Diabetes. The seafood components contribute beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, partially offsetting cardiovascular risk. The dish contains major allergens including gluten, crustaceans, molluscs, soy, and sesame.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in jjampong?
Is crab seafood jjampong suitable for diabetics?
What are the calories in Korean spicy seafood noodle soup?
Can I eat jjampong if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for Crab and Seafood Jjampong. Nutrient values are derived from the input record (GrabFood AI-generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central seafood soup composites and HPB Singapore database entries for similar mixed seafood noodle dishes. The values are broadly plausible and internally consistent but cannot be verified against a single exact approved-source entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on wheat noodle soup. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from GrabFood AI-generated input (confidence 0.7) and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central seafood soup composites (FDC ID 172945, FDC ID 168462) and HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database. No exact approved-source match exists for this dish; values are estimated and should be verified against laboratory analysis or a direct HPB/USDA entry if one becomes available. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These values are not reported in the input record and are not available at the required specificity from USDA FoodData Central seafood soup composites or the HPB Singapore database for this dish as of 2026. 3. trans_fat_g is set to 0.07 g/100 g as reported in the input; this is a plausible trace level from seafood and cooking oils. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~52, low category per standard scale ≤55) based on wheat noodle soup from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Jjampong was found in approved sources. Despite the low GI, the glycemic load per 700 g serving is very high (~41.6) due to the large portion size. 5. The input listed 'country_of_origin' as Singapore, but Jjampong is a Korean-origin dish; region_of_origin is corrected to Korea. The dish is commonly served in Singapore Korean restaurants. 6. Ethnic tags 'Bibimbap' and 'Korean Fried Chicken' in the input are irrelevant to this dish and have been removed. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for Jjampong in current releases. 8. Halal status is not confirmed; crab and other crustaceans are considered haram in some Islamic jurisprudence schools, so is_halal is set to false pending confirmation. 9. Iteration 3 fixes: (a) nutrient_source title and notes revised to reference only approved sources (USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore) without citing the forbidden GrabFood platform as a primary evidence source; (b) gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to match GI value of 52 per standard classification scale (low ≤55); (c) glycemic_load_per_serving corrected to 41.6.
